name: Dal Makhani
tagline: Slow-simmered black lentils, butter-kissed and rich

hero.alt: Dal makhani in a cream ceramic bowl with cream swirl and melting butter
hero.caption: dal makhani — slow-simmered lentils, cream, butter

healthFacts: Black urad dal delivers ~18g plant protein per cup — higher than most other lentils.
The slow simmer breaks down phytates, making the iron in lentils more bioavailable.
Kidney beans (rajma) add fiber that feeds beneficial gut bacteria.
Butter contains butyrate — a short-chain fatty acid that supports gut lining health.
Ginger and cumin in the tempering have well-documented anti-inflammatory properties.
A lower-fat version: use 1 tbsp butter instead of 3 and stir in 2 tbsp yogurt off-heat.

steps.title: Soak overnight
steps.detail: Wash the urad dal and rajma. Soak together in 3 cups of water for at least 8 hours, or overnight. They'll roughly double in size. Drain and rinse before cooking.
steps.tip: Soaking reduces cooking time significantly and breaks down gas-causing compounds.

media.alt: Black urad dal and rajma soaking together in water
media.caption: Overnight soak

steps.title: Pressure cook the lentils
steps.detail: Add soaked dal + rajma to a pressure cooker with 3 cups fresh water and 1 tsp salt. Cook on high for 6–7 whistles (or Instant Pot: 30 min high pressure). Dal should be completely soft and mushy — mash a grain between your fingers to check.
steps.tip: Dal Makhani's creaminess comes from partially mashed dal. Don't drain the cooking water.

media.alt: Soft pressure-cooked urad dal and rajma in starchy cooking water
media.caption: Soft cooked lentils

steps.title: Make the tomato base
steps.detail: In butter, temper cumin seeds until they splutter. Add finely chopped onion and cook 7 minutes until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook 2 minutes. Add pureed tomatoes, chili powder, coriander powder, and cook until the masala deepens in color and oil separates.
steps.tip: The masala is ready when you see the fat pooling at the edges of the pan.

media.alt: Tomato-onion masala reducing in butter with oil separating at the edges
media.caption: Jammy tomato base

steps.title: Combine and simmer
steps.detail: Add the cooked dal (with all its water) to the masala. Mix gently. The dal should look like a thick, dark soup. Partially mash some of the lentils with the back of the ladle against the pot wall for creaminess.
steps.tip: The more you mash, the creamier the final texture. Aim for about 30% mashed.

media.alt: Cooked lentils and rajma stirred into tomato masala with a ladle
media.caption: Dal folded into masala

steps.title: Low and slow
steps.detail: Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to the lowest possible setting. Simmer uncovered for 20–25 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes to prevent sticking. The dal thickens and gets darker and more complex as it cooks.
steps.tip: Restaurant dal makhani simmers for 4–6 hours. 20 minutes is the home minimum.

media.alt: Dal makhani simmering uncovered with slow bubbles in a black kadhai
media.caption: Low, slow simmer

steps.title: Butter and cream finish
steps.detail: Stir in 1 tbsp cold butter and the cream. Add garam masala. Taste and adjust salt. Let it sit covered for 5 minutes before serving. Garnish with a swirl of cream and a small nub of butter.
steps.tip: Cold butter stirred in off-heat gives a glossy, restaurant-like finish.

media.alt: Glossy dal makhani finished with cream, butter, and a brass ladle
media.caption: Butter and cream finish
